title: John Swinney's talk of potential US military ban at Prestwick is worst kind of political grandstanding

sections.heading: Column's main criticism
sections.content: In a Scotsman opinion column, Martyn McLaughlin described John Swinney's talk of a potential ban on US military use of Prestwick Airport as the worst kind of political grandstanding. The criticism followed Swinney's statement that he would consider restrictions if evidence showed US aircraft using Prestwick had been involved in offensive operations in the Middle East.

sections.heading: Why the column calls it inconsistent
sections.content: McLaughlin argued that successive SNP governments had long treated Prestwick as an arm's-length commercial operation and said ministers had used that position to avoid responsibility for difficult questions about US military traffic. He said the tougher language from Swinney therefore sat uneasily with the government's earlier insistence that ministers did not intervene in the airport's commercial decisions and only added to the sense of inconsistency.

sections.heading: Prestwick's finances in the argument
sections.content: The column also argued that Prestwick depends heavily on US military fuel sales, and questioned whether a practical ban would ever follow the First Minister's rhetoric.

sourceReferences.citationText: serper-scottish-aviation – John Swinney's talk of potential US military ban at Prestwick is political grandstanding
